authorship_evidence: Project created in 2021 by Gabor Seljan, consistent commit history by a single human author, pre-dates widespread LLM code generation

evidence.description: SQL injection via string concatenation in SQLinjection.run(). The 'id' parameter from request is concatenated directly into SQL: cursor.execute("SELECT ... WHERE id=" + id). No parameterization or input validation.

SQL injection in AuthBypass.run() via the password parameter. The password is concatenated directly into the login query: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"). While username is sanitized with re.sub(r"[^\w]", '', ...), the password parameter has no sanitization, enabling authentication bypass via SQLi payloads like ' OR '1'='1.

Reflected XSS in XSSReflected.run(). The 'msg' parameter from the request is returned directly as HTML content without any escaping or sanitization: content = content[0]. Injecting <script>alert(1)</script> as the msg parameter executes arbitrary JavaScript.

Stored XSS in XSSStored.run(). Comments retrieved from the database are rendered into HTML table cells without escaping: columns += "{}".format(column). Although comments are stored via parameterized INSERT (preventing SQLi), no output encoding is applied when rendering, so stored XSS payloads execute for every visitor.

Open redirect in UnvalidatedRedirect.run(). The 'path' parameter is injected directly into a JavaScript window.location.replace() call via Python string formatting: window.location.replace('{path}'). An attacker can redirect users to arbitrary URLs. Additionally, since the value is injected into a <script> context, a payload like ');alert(1);// achieves XSS.

OS command injection in CommandInjection.run(). The 'domain' parameter is concatenated with the command name and passed to subprocess.check_output() with shell=True: subprocess.check_output(' '.join([command, domain]), shell=True, ...). Payloads like 'google.com;whoami' execute arbitrary commands. Requires risk level 3.

Insecure deserialization via pickle in UnsafeDeserialization.run(). User-supplied base64-encoded data is decoded and passed directly to pickle.loads(): pickle.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(object)). A crafted pickle payload with __reduce__ achieves arbitrary code execution. Requires risk level 3.

Path traversal in PathTraversal.run(). The 'path' parameter is passed to open() after os.path.abspath() which does NOT prevent traversal: file = open(os.path.abspath(path), 'rb'). A payload like '../../etc/passwd' reads arbitrary files. The only check is whether '://' is in the path (which routes to SSRF instead).

SSRF in PathTraversal.run(). When the 'path' parameter contains '://', it is passed directly to urllib.request.urlopen(path), allowing the attacker to make requests to arbitrary URLs including internal services (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/ for cloud metadata).

Session fixation in SessionFixation.run(). The 'session' parameter from the request is directly set as the SESSIONID cookie: cookie['SESSIONID'] = session. An attacker can send a victim a link with a pre-set session ID, then hijack the session after the victim authenticates.

Execution After Redirect (EAR) in ExecutionAfterRedirect.run(). The /admin page renders sensitive admin content (Manage Users, Update Database Settings links) before checking authentication. The redirect to /login is done via client-side JavaScript (window.location), which can be blocked by the browser or intercepted, allowing unauthenticated users to see admin page content.

Cross-Site Request Forgery in XSRequestForgery.run(). The settings form at /settings uses GET method with no CSRF token. An attacker can craft a link or image tag that changes a user's email address: /settings?email=attacker@evil.com. The state-changing operation (email update) has no anti-CSRF protection.

Clickjacking vulnerability in Clickjacking.run(). The /danger endpoint performs account deletion via GET request with no CSRF token and no X-Frame-Options header. The page can be embedded in an iframe on a malicious site, tricking users into clicking the delete button. The destructive action (DELETE FROM users) is triggered by a simple GET with ?delete=1.

Critical command injection via custom HTTP method BDR in do_BDR(). The URL path (self.path[1:]) is passed directly to subprocess.check_output() with shell=True: subprocess.check_output(self.path[1:], shell=True, ...). Any command can be executed by sending a BDR HTTP request with the command as the URL path. Requires risk level 3.

DOM-based XSS in custom.js. The URL fragment (document.location.hash) is decoded and injected directly into the page via document.write() without sanitization: document.write('' + decodeURIComponent(document.location.hash.substring(index + 5)) + ''). A URL like /#lang= executes arbitrary JavaScript. This affects every page that includes custom.js.

Hardcoded backdoor credentials in AuthBypass.run(). The code contains a hardcoded check: if username == 'dsvpwa' and password == 'dsvpwa', which bypasses the database authentication entirely. This is a backdoor account that grants access regardless of database contents.

Plaintext credentials stored in users.xml data file. All user passwords are stored in cleartext including admin (Y3RFgwZP$v6v#D), guest (guest), loella (babygirl), and weitzel (123456). This file is used to seed the database at startup.

Information disclosure in SQLinjection.run(). SQLite OperationalError exceptions are caught and returned directly to the user: return e. This reveals database schema information, table names, and query structure to attackers, aiding further SQL injection exploitation.

Information disclosure in AuthBypass.run(). SQLite OperationalError exceptions from the login query are caught and returned to the user: return content.format(type=type, message=e). This reveals database error details including table/column names and query structure.

Insecure SSL/TLS configuration in main(). Three dangerous SSL options are explicitly enabled: (1) SSLv3 re-enabled via ctx.options &= ~ssl.OP_NO_SSLv3 (vulnerable to POODLE), (2) TLS compression re-enabled via &= ~ssl.OP_NO_COMPRESSION (vulnerable to CRIME), (3) cipher server preference disabled via &= ~ssl.OP_CIPHER_SERVER_PREFERENCE (allows client to choose weak ciphers).

Session cookies set without HttpOnly or Secure flags. At line 234, cookies are output via morsel.OutputString() without setting httponly or secure attributes. Only 'path' is set to '/'. This allows JavaScript to access session cookies (enabling session theft via XSS) and cookies to be transmitted over unencrypted connections.

XSS protection explicitly disabled via response header. X-XSS-Protection is set to '0', disabling the browser's built-in XSS filter. While modern browsers have deprecated this header, disabling it removes a defense-in-depth layer in older browsers.

Weak Content-Security-Policy header. CSP is set to "default-src * 'unsafe-inline'" which allows loading resources from any origin and inline script execution, effectively providing no protection against XSS attacks. This is intentionally weak to enable the XSS attack vectors.

Login form uses GET method: <form id="login" method="GET" action="/login">. Credentials (username and password) are transmitted as URL query parameters, which are logged in browser history, server access logs, proxy logs, and Referer headers. Passwords should never be sent via GET.

Passwords stored in plaintext in the database. User passwords from users.xml are inserted directly into SQLite without hashing: INSERT INTO users(..., password, ...) VALUES(NULL, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?). The password column in the users table (line 33) stores raw plaintext passwords, making all credentials recoverable if the database is compromised.

Weak session token generation using Python's random module instead of cryptographically secure alternatives. Session tokens are generated with random.sample(string.ascii_letters + string.digits, 20), which uses the Mersenne Twister PRNG. This is predictable and not suitable for security-sensitive operations. Should use secrets.token_urlsafe() or os.urandom().

Open redirect in SessionFixation.run(). The 'path' parameter is injected into a JavaScript window.location assignment without validation: window.location = '{path}'. This is a secondary vulnerability in the session fixation handler - in addition to fixing the session, the redirect destination is also attacker-controlled.

Path traversal in StaticHandler.find(). The URL path is used directly in file open calls: open('./{}'.format(path), 'rb') and open('./{}'.format(path), 'r'). No path sanitization or directory restriction is applied. Requests like /static/../../etc/passwd (with an appropriate extension to bypass route matching) read arbitrary files from the filesystem.

The AuthBypass.run() handler at /login has no rate limiting or account lockout mechanism, allowing unlimited brute-force login attempts. The login form also uses GET method, exposing credentials in URLs and server logs. No existing ground truth entry covers the absence of brute-force protection on this endpoint — dsvpwa-002 covers SQL injection in the same handler, and dsvpwa-024 covers GET-method credential exposure in the template, but neither addresses the missing rate limiting.

The 'cookie' attribute is defined as a class variable on VulnHTTPRequestHandler (line 141), meaning it is shared across all request handler instances. In Python's http.server framework, this causes one user's session cookie state to leak to another concurrent user's request, enabling session confusion/hijacking. This is distinct from dsvpwa-010 (session fixation via user-controlled parameter), dsvpwa-021 (missing HttpOnly flag), and dsvpwa-026 (weak PRNG for token generation).

The global exception handler in do_GET at handlers.py lines 216-218 sends raw exception messages to the client via 'content = str(ex)'. This can expose internal implementation details including file paths, module names, and exception types from any unhandled exception during request processing. This is a separate sink from the CWE-209 entries in ground truth (dsvpwa-018 at attacks.py:40-41 and dsvpwa-019 at attacks.py:290-291), which cover specific SQLite error disclosure in individual attack handlers.

The guestbook comment form at templates/guestbook.html:27 uses method='GET' with no anti-CSRF token, allowing an attacker to trick a user into submitting arbitrary comments via a crafted link (e.g., /guestbook?comment=malicious). This is a distinct CSRF from dsvpwa-012 which covers the /settings endpoint (email change) and dsvpwa-013 which covers /danger (account deletion). Lower impact than those entries but a genuine, independently exploitable CSRF on a state-changing operation.

NOT VULNERABLE. XSSStored.run() uses parameterized query for INSERT: cursor.execute('INSERT INTO comments VALUES(NULL, ?, ?)', [comment, time.ctime()]). The ? placeholders properly bind the user-supplied comment value, preventing SQL injection. The stored XSS vulnerability exists in the output rendering (line 97), not in the database insertion.

NOT VULNERABLE. SessionHijacking.run() uses parameterized query: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE session = ?", [session]). The session value from the cookie is bound via placeholder, preventing SQL injection.

NOT VULNERABLE. XSRequestForgery.run() uses parameterized query for SELECT: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE session = ?", [session]). The session cookie value is properly bound via placeholder.

NOT VULNERABLE. XSRequestForgery.run() uses parameterized query for UPDATE: cursor.execute("UPDATE users SET email = ? WHERE id = ?", (email, user[0])). Both the email value and user ID are bound via placeholders.
NOT VULNERABLE. Clickjacking.run() uses parameterized query for SELECT: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE session = ?", [session]). The session cookie value is properly bound.

NOT VULNERABLE (for the username parameter). AuthBypass.run() sanitizes the username with re.sub(r"[^\w]", '', params.get('username')[0]), stripping all non-word characters. This prevents SQL injection through the username parameter. Note: the password parameter on line 289 IS vulnerable to SQL injection.
